Solomon speaks with the urgency of a father watching his son walk toward a cliff he cannot yet see. The forbidden woman appears first as pure attraction—her words drip honey, smoother than oil—but this chapter refuses to let us stay in that seductive moment. We are forced to follow the trajectory to its actual end: bitterness, sharpness, death, Sheol. The proverb does not argue that forbidden desire is wrong because it feels wrong. It argues that forbidden desire is wrong because it *leads* somewhere specific, and that destination is ruin. By verse 11, we hear the voice of a man at life's end, groaning in regret, finally understanding what he despised when it mattered—the discipline and reproof that could have saved him. This is not abstract moralizing. This is the sound of a life spent.

But notice what this chapter actually teaches about desire itself. Solomon does not tell his son to kill his appetite for beauty, intimacy, or passionate love. Instead, he redirects it. "Drink water from your own cistern, and running water from your own well." The image is not one of deprivation but of satisfaction—your own springs, your own fountain, blessed and abundant. He calls the wife of youth "a loving doe, a graceful fawn" and asks that her breasts "satisfy you always" and that you "be captivated by her love forever." This is not the language of duty or obligation. It is the language of desire fulfilled within its proper bounds. The point is not that sexual longing is sin; the point is that sexual longing *belongs* somewhere specific, in covenant, in blessing, in a relationship meant to last a lifetime. Everything forbidden offers itself as more, but it delivers less. Everything permitted within God's design offers itself humbly but delivers infinitely more.

The final turn is decisive: "A man's ways are before the eyes of the LORD, and the LORD examines all his paths." This is not a threat meant to shame you into obedience through fear of surveillance. It is an invitation to remember who is actually watching, and what He actually wants for you. The man who entangles himself in sin, who dies for lack of discipline, has chosen his own great folly over the gaze of the God who knows every step. You cannot hide from Him. But more than that—you do not need to. He has already shown you the path of life. He has already blessed the fountain that satisfies. The question is whether you will believe Him enough to turn away from what glitters and toward what actually sustains.
